results.possibleIdentity: Edward Botwinick (1936–2025) was a prominent American electrical engineer, entrepreneur, and pioneer in the information technology and data communications industries. He earned a BA in physics (1956) and a BS in electrical engineering (1958) from Columbia University. His early career included roles at US Semiconductor Corp. and co-founding Silicon Transistor Corp. in 1960. From 1963 to 1967, he served as the president and principal shareholder of Quantum Inc., a New York-based manufacturer of magnetic tape drives and scalable file storage systems. Following his tenure at Quantum, he joined Goldman Sachs as vice president of high-technology investment research and venture capital (1967–1977). In 1969, he co-founded Timeplex, Inc., where he served as chairman and CEO from 1977 until 1988, leading the company to become a dominant force in time-division multiplexing (TDM) and T1-based networking technologies. After Timeplex was acquired by Unisys Corp. in 1988, Botwinick became a senior vice president and president of Unisys Networks. He later founded VideoServer Inc. in 1991 and served as its chairman and CEO. A major benefactor to Columbia Engineering, he funded the Botwinick Gateway Laboratory, a multimedia electronic learning facility dedicated in 1996. He was also an active pilot and served on several boards, including the Lamont-Doherty Earth Observatory and the Duke Cancer Institute.

segment.text: His early career included roles at US Semiconductor Corp. and co-founding Silicon Transistor Corp. in 1960.
Following his tenure at Quantum, he joined Goldman Sachs as vice president of high-technology investment research and venture capital (1967–1977).

In 1969, he co-founded Timeplex, Inc., where he served as chairman and CEO from 1977 until 1988, leading the company to become a dominant force in time-division multiplexing (TDM) and T1-based networking technologies.
After Timeplex was acquired by Unisys Corp. in 1988, Botwinick became a senior vice president and president of Unisys Networks.
He later founded VideoServer Inc. in 1991 and served as its chairman and CEO.
A major benefactor to Columbia Engineering, he funded the Botwinick Gateway Laboratory, a multimedia electronic learning facility dedicated in 1996.
